coredata.dc:title: Tunable KLa(MoO4)2:Eu3+@CDs composite materials for white LED and multi-mode information encryption technology
coredata.dc:creator: Lv, Ziqian
Xu, Hanyu
Wang, Zhiying
Jia, Mochen
Wei, Yanling
Fu, Zuoling
coredata.dc:description: The composite of biocompatible carbon dots (CDs) and lanthanide-based luminescent materials have high research value as a promising multifunctional material, which are ideally suited to information encryption and white LED. However, the aggregation-induced quenching of CDs in the solid state still troubles the synthesis of CDs with other solid luminescent materials. Herein, a facile strategy to fabricate polychromatic nanoplatform KLa(MoO4)2: Eu3+@CDs by encapsulating CDs on the surface of KLM: Eu3+ with silicon shell is reported, in which the aggregation-induced quenching of CDs is avoided. Due to the overlapping of Eu-O charge transfer band with CDs excitation band, the red luminescence of Eu3+ ions and the blue fluorescence of can be emitted simultaneously under 395 nm excitation. By changing the proportion of KLa(MoO4)2: Eu3+ in KLa(MoO4)2: Eu3+@CDs, the mixture can emit "warm", "neutral", and "cold" white light, which can realize people's demand for indoor lighting requirements. Furthermore, a multi-mode information encryption by selectively triggering the luminescence of CDs or Eu3+ ions is also designed. All the results suggest that KLa(MoO4)2: Eu3+@CDs have excellent luminescence properties that can be further investigated and applied in the field of white LEDs and information encryption.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Excitation-dependent
Composite materials
Optical materials
Chemical synthesis
Optical properties

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images (the inset: HRTEM image), and (b) size distribution of CDs. (c) TEM images of KLM: Eu3+ (the inset: HRTEM image of KLM: Eu3+). (d) TEM images of KLM: Eu3+@CDs (the inset: HRTEM image of KLM: Eu3+@CDs). (e) XRD patterns and (f) FT-IR spectra of CDs, KLM: Eu3+ and KLM: Eu3+@CDs.

(a) The normalized UV–vis absorbance (black dashed line), emission (red line), and excitation (blue line) spectra of the CDs. (b) Photographs of CDs solution under sunlight and turning on 365 nm UV light. (c) Emission spectra of CDs under the excitation from 300 nm to 460 nm and (d) Photographs of CDs powder under day-light, before and after turning off 365 nm UV light.
PL spectra of (a) KLM: xEu3+, (b) KLM: xEu3+ at pH = 6–9 and (c) KLM: xEu3+(x = 0.1–0.6). (d) XRD patterns of KLM: xEu3+(x = 0.1–0.6).
(a) Excitation spectrum at 614 nm of KLM: Eu3+@CDs. (b) Emission spectrum at 284 nm, (c)354 nm, and (d) 395 nm of KLM: Eu3+@CDs. (e) CIE chromaticity coordinates at 284 nm, 354 nm, and 395 nm.
Schematic illustration of the PL and afterglow emission mechanisms for CDs and KLM: Eu3+ energy transfer scheme in KLM: Eu3+@CDs phosphors.
(a) PL spectra and (b) CIE chromaticity coordinates of KLM: Eu3+@CDs (KLM: Eu3+: CDs = 0:1, 1:2, 1:1, 2:1, 10:1, and 1:0).

(a) W-LED and information protection applications of the KLM: Eu3+@CDs. (a) CIE chromaticity coordinates of W-LED (the inset: the photograph of W-LED). (b) Design of information protection. (c)The pattern under the excitation 254 nm, 365 nm, and 395 nm UV lamp on.

head.ce:title: Tunable KLa(MoO4)2:Eu3+@CDs composite materials for white LED and multi-mode information encryption technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Coherent Light and Atomic and Molecular Spectroscopy Laboratory, Key Laboratory of physics and Technology for Advanced Batteries, College of Physics, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Coherent Light and Atomic and Molecular Spectroscopy Laboratory, Key Laboratory of physics and Technology for Advanced Batteries, College of Physics, Jilin University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Coherent Light and Atomic and Molecular Spectroscopy Laboratory, Key Laboratory of physics and Technology for Advanced Batteries, College of Physics, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of applied Sciences, Jilin Engineering Normal University, Changchun 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of applied Sciences, Jilin Engineering Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of applied Sciences, Jilin Engineering Normal University, Changchun, 130012, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The composite of biocompatible carbon dots (CDs) and lanthanide-based luminescent materials have high research value as a promising multifunctional material, which are ideally suited to information encryption and white LED. However, the aggregation-induced quenching of CDs in the solid state still troubles the synthesis of CDs with other solid luminescent materials. Herein, a facile strategy to fabricate polychromatic nanoplatform KLa(MoO4)2: Eu3+@CDs by encapsulating CDs on the surface of KLM: Eu3+ with silicon shell is reported, in which the aggregation-induced quenching of CDs is avoided. Due to the overlapping of Eu-O charge transfer band with CDs excitation band, the red luminescence of Eu3+ ions and the blue fluorescence of can be emitted simultaneously under 395 nm excitation. By changing the proportion of KLa(MoO4)2: Eu3+ in KLa(MoO4)2: Eu3+@CDs, the mixture can emit "warm", "neutral", and "cold" white light, which can realize people's demand for indoor lighting requirements. Furthermore, a multi-mode information encryption by selectively triggering the luminescence of CDs or Eu3+ ions is also designed. All the results suggest that KLa(MoO4)2: Eu3+@CDs have excellent luminescence properties that can be further investigated and applied in the field of white LEDs and information encryption.

ce:section.ce:para: With the development of science and technology, information technology is widely used, and information security has become more and more important to people [1]. The transmission and preservation of many confidential data urgently need high security level information encryption technology. Because of the advantages of simple production process, high visibility and good color stability of optical encryption materials, optical encryption technology has been developed rapidly in recent years and become one of the important research contents of modern encryption technology [2–6]. As a new type of fluoresce-based nanoparticles, CDs hold the promise of revolutionizing information encryption due to its unique optical properties, high water solubility and non-toxicity [7–10]. CDs are usually spherical nanoparticles with a regular particle size of less than 10 nm, with an abundance of organic functional groups on the surface, which have the potential to bind to other materials [11]. Under UV lamp excitation, the luminescent color of CDs is usually blue or green, and its emission peak is generally a broadband peak with a width greater than 200 nm [12]. Therefore, CDs, which have been popularly concerned by people in recent years, have a cheerful application prospect in sensing, biological imaging, photodynamic therapy, optoelectronic devices, anti-counterfeiting, and so on [13,14]. However, its application in multicolor displays and W-LED is limited by the fact that CDs normally have a single luminescent color lacking orange-red luminescence and the luminescence is prone to quench in the solid state [15].

In recent decades, lanthanum-based luminescent materials have greatly attracted attention, because of their excellent light stability, rich energy level structure and wide range of luminescent wavelengths, and they have a wide range of research and applications in W-LED, anti-counterfeiting, and bio-imaging fields [16–19]. Many of these have good solid state luminescence properties [20,21]. If it is organically combined with CDs, the luminescence quenching of CDs possibly may be avoided, and a new composite material with dual-mode luminescence may be developed. This idea has proven to be feasible, and there have been recent attempts to investigate the compounding of rare earth luminescent materials with CDs [22,23]. For example, Desai et al. synthesized Eu3+ hybridized CDs by microwave heating of L-cysteine and EDTA [24]. Li et al. grew CDs on the surfaces of NaYF4:Er3+,Yb3+ and prepared composites with simultaneous up- and down-conversion dual-mode luminescence for multi-mode anti-counterfeiting [25]. Xu et al. combined NaYF4:Eu3+ with CDs to prepare multi-color luminescent composites that can be applied in ion detection, temperature measurement, and white LEDs [26]. Although great efforts have been made, the composite schemes and multifunctional applications of these composites are still in their infancy and further research is urgently needed.

At present, when lanthanide luminescent materials are combined with CDs, NaYF4 has invariably been used as the carrier of lanthanide elements [27–29]. Compared to fluoride, oxygenated compounds have been more widely studied because of their stability and simplicity of synthesis, and they contain charge migration bands that are not present in the presence of fluoride [30]. The composites formed by combining oxygenated salts with CDs may show more diverse luminescence patterns. In this study, KLa(MoO4)2: Eu3+ and CDs were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method, and combined them to form KLa(MoO4)2: Eu3+@CDs (KLM: Eu3+@CDs) composites by silicon shell coating [31]. KLM: Eu3+@CDs can respectively show blue, red and white luminescence under different wavelengths of UV excitation. Interestingly, by altering the ratio of KLM: Eu3+ and CDs in the synthesis process, the luminescent color of KLM: Eu3+@CDs is adjustable and KLM: Eu3+@CDs can emit "warm", "neutral", and "cold" white luminescence, which could be made into white LED to meet people's various needs for indoor lighting [32]. In addition, based on the multi-mode luminescence behavior of KLM: Eu3+@CDs, it is used in the information encryption applications [33,34].

The original material Eu2O3 were weighed stoichiometrically and dissolved in HNO3 (Labkem,65%). (NH4)6Mo7O24∙4H2O was used as the source of molybdate. Reagent grade of ethylenediamine tetracetic acid (EDTA), ethylenediamine (EDA) and La(NO3)3∙H2O were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd (Beijing, China), H3PO4 was obtained from Beijing Chemical Reagent Plant (Beijing, China). All the initial chemicals were of analytical grade. Deionized (DI) water were used throughout this work.

The KLM: Eu3+ were prepared by a similar route to the literature [35]. Typically, 0.6 mmol of La(NO3)3, 0.4 mmol Eu(NO3)3 and 0.20 g of EDTA were dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water (DI water) to vigorous stirring for 20 min to form solution A. 2/7 mmol (NH4)6Mo7O24∙4H2O was dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water, and vigorous stirring for 30 min to form solution B. After stirring, solution B was slowly dropped into solution A under vigorous stirring for 10 min. Before the pH value of the mixture was adjusted to 7 by dropping in KOH (5 M) solution, the mixture was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon bottle in a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and hydrothermally heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After the autoclave was naturally cooled to room temperature, the resulting precursors was separated by centrifugation for 10 min with a speed of 11000 rpm/min. Finally, the sample was washed with water and alcohol for three times and dried at 60 °C for 12 h to obtain the finished product.

4 mL of EDA and 16 mL of DI water were added to the beaker, then 8 mL of H3PO4 was slowly added dropwise while stirring, and the solution was then transferred to flask. The solution was first evaporated at 100 °C for 2 h to remove the water, and then heated at 280 °C for 2 h in a nitrogen atmosphere. After cooling, the crude product was dissolved in deionized water, centrifuged at 11000 rpm/min for 10 min, and then filtered through a 0.22 µm filter to remove insoluble large particles. The obtained light brown solution was dialyzed in dialysis bags (3500 D) with deionized water for 1 day to remove small molecules. Finally, after adding ethanol as eluent to the solution, the supernatant was collected and awaited further utilization.

0.2 g KLM: Eu3+ were added to 4 mL of silica sol, which is mixed by TEOS, HCl(pH = 2), EtOH and H2O with molar ratio of 1:0.2:4:5. Afterward, 1 mL of CDs solution was injected into the mixed solution. The mixed solution was heated by condensation reflux at 80 °C for 4 h, and washed three times with ethanol. Finally, the KLM: Eu3+@CDs composites were obtained after drying the sample at 60 °C for 12 h.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) were obtained by JEM-2200FS Field Emission Electron Microscope. Fourier transformed infrared (FT-IR) spectra of CDs were measured using a Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrometer. The crystal phase of the samples was identified by X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15405 nm) with a SmartLab SE diffractometer. The photoluminescence (PL) excitation and emission spectra were obtained using an Andor Shamrock SR-750 fluorescence spectrometer equipped with a xenon lamp (150 W) as an excitation source. The UV–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were tested by using a Shimadzu UV-1750 UV–vis spectrophotometer.

By comparing the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) in Fig. 1, The microstructure characteristics of the composites and two raw materials can be obtained. As shown in Fig. 1a, the spherical CDs particles are uniformly dispersed without aggregation. The high-resolution (HR) TEM in the inset demonstrated that the lattice spacing of CDs between the fringes is 0.21 nm corresponding to the (1 0 0) planes of grapheme [36]. This indicates that the CDs synthesized by us possess carbonization cores, which will result in the luminescence of CDs. The particle size distribution of CDs ranged from 2.0 to 6.7 nm, and the average particle size was calculated to be 4.6 nm as shown in Fig. 1b. The average size of ellipsoidal KLM: Eu3+ with uniform size and regular shape is 2 µm (Fig. 1c). It can be seen from the HRTEM in the inset of Fig. 1c that there are lattice fringes a spacing of 0.32 nm on the KLM: Eu3+ corresponding to (1 2 −1) planes, which illustrates that KLM: Eu3+ is a monoclinic crystal with regular structure. From TEM of KLM: Eu3+@CDs (Fig. 1d), it can be further found that there are lattice fringes with the spacing of 0.32 nm and 0.21 nm (the inset of Fig. 1d), which correspond to the (1 2 −1) planes of KLM: Eu3+ and (1 0 0) planes of CDs, respectively. The KLM: Eu3+@CDs composites have been successfully prepared by coating CDs on the surface of KLM: Eu3+.

As shown in Fig. 1e, the hump peak at around 2θ = 22.5° of the CDs in the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns is attributed to the (0 0 2) crystal plane of highly disordered carbon atoms. All diffraction peaks of KLM can be accurately labeled as monoclinic crystalline phase of KLa(MoO4)2 (PDF no. 40–0466) without any impurities. The hump peak of CDs and the characteristic diffraction peak of KLa(MoO4)2 are co-existed in the XRD patterns of KLM: Eu3+@CDs composites, which is consistent with the observation of HRTEM.

Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) is used to characterize the chemical structure and composition of the samples. As seen in Fig. 1f, a large absorption band of 2500–3700 cm−1 (containing distinguishable peaks at 3445, 3253, 2983, and 2931 cm−1) is presented in the FT-IR spectrum of the CDs attributed to the stretching vibrations of methylene (-CH2), amino (-NH2), and hydroxyl (-OH) groups. The peaks located at 1642 cm−1 correspond to the bending vibration of -CC-, respectively. The broad absorption band at 1082 cm-1 should be caused by Si-O-Si asymmetric stretching and P-O-C vibrations [37]. The weak absorption signal at 571 and 3445 cm−1 indicated the presence of amine and phosphate salts. By comparing the FT-IR absorption curves of CDs, KLM: Eu3+ and KLM: Eu3+@CDs, there are C-OH, -CH2- and CN on the surface of KLM: Eu3+. This is because EDTA acts as a chelating agent to make the two materials more easily compounded, allowing the presence of functional groups on the surface of KLM: Eu3+. By contrast, the absorption peaks of -CH2- and C-H on the surface of KLM: Eu3+@CDs composites are weakened, because the functional groups on the KLM: Eu3+ surface are covered by the Si-coated shell. The enhancement of C-OH and -CH- absorption peaks and the appearance of Si-O-Si and phosphate absorption peaks indicate the presence of CDs in the silica shell on the surface of KLM: Eu3+@CDs composites material. From the above results, it can be determined that the composite material was successfully prepared with KLM: Eu3+ as the core and CDs in the outer layer.

Fig. 2a demonstrates the normalized PL spectra and UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, which were studied for the luminescence properties of CDs. The UV-Vis absorption spectrum of CDs solution has distinct absorption peaks, one at 280 nm and the other in the range of 320–400 nm which may be attributed to the π→π* of the conjugated CC band and n→π * transitions of C–NC or CO functional groups [38]. The optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of CDs are 380 and 440 nm, respectively. Moreover, the excitation spectrum is consistent with the UV–vis absorption peak at 340 nm, which evidences that the absorption of CDs at 340 nm leads to its fluorescence. The CDs solution is pale yellow under natural light and will emit blue luminescence under the stimulation of a 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 2b). The CDs will exhibit strong excitation-dependent behavior, whose emission peak will shift from 410 nm to 520 nm with the variation of excitation wavelengths (Fig. 2c). This illustrates existence of multiple emission channels in the CDs, which can be caused by abundant surface functional groups of CDs. The CDs powder is brown but does not emit light when excited at 365 nm. However, after coating the silicon shell, the solid CDs turn white, when it is irradiated by a 365 nm UV lamp, the blue luminescence will be exhibited as shown in Fig. 2d. The appearance of silicon shell separates the originally aggregated CDs from each other, thus avoiding aggregation-induced quenching. Otherwise, the stable excited triplet species in CDs will result in green afterglow.

The luminescent properties of KLM: Eu3+ are shown in Fig. 3a. When the KLM: Eu3+ is excited by 395 nm, the emission peaks of Eu3+ at 592 nm, 614 nm, 651 nm, and 703 nm can be detected. Comparing the emission peaks of KLM: Eu3+ located at 592 nm and 615 nm, the emission peak at 615 nm is strongest, which indicates that Eu3+ mainly occupies the center of non-inversion symmetry and is dominated by 5D0-7F2 electric dipole transition. Furthermore, when KLM: Eu3+ is monitored at 616 nm, the whole excitation spectrum is divided into two excitation bands. The charge transfer band of Eu-O leads to the present of the excitation band which is located at 284 nm and ranges from 230 nm to 450 nm. The other one consists of several narrow bands, whose peaks are located at 364 nm, 382 nm, 395 nm and 416 nm, which correspond to the F–F transitions of Eu3+ from the ground state 7F0 to the excited state 5G2 (376 nm, 381 nm), 5L6 and 5D2, respectively [39].

Moreover, the optimum synthesis condition of KLM: Eu3+ is also investigated. Firstly, the emission spectra of KLM: Eu3+ prepared under pH from 6 to 9 are shown in the Fig. 3b. Increasing the value of pH, the luminescent intensity first increases and then decreases. When the value of pH reaches 7, the luminescent intensity is strongest. Therefore, 7 is the best synthetic pH value of KLM: Eu3+. Next, for exploring the optimal doping concentration of Eu3+ in KLM, the emission spectra of KLM: xEu3+ (x = 0.10, 0.20, 0.30, 0.40, 0.50, and 0.60) are shown in Fig. 3c. It can be seen that with the increase of Eu3+ ion doping concentration, the luminescent intensity of Eu3+ first increases and then decreases when the doping concentration reaches at 0.40. After the concentration of Eu3+ ion more than 0.40, the crystal structure of KLa(MoO4)2 is destroyed, which leads to the decrease of luminescence intensity (Fig. 3d).

The excitation spectrum of the KLM: 0.40Eu3+@CDs monitored at 614 nm is shown in Fig. 4a. There are the strongest excitation peaks at 284 nm and 395 nm, and the excitation peak at 395 nm is weaker. Because of the photons will not only excite Eu3+ but also be absorbed by CDs, the excitation peak of Eu3+ is attenuated under 395 nm excitation. Then, the sample is excited at 284 nm, 354 nm and 395 nm, which emits red, blue and white fluorescence respectively, and the spectra as shown in Fig. 4c-e. As expected, KLM: Eu3+@CDs composites maintain the characteristic luminescence of Eu3+ ion and CDs, showing the red luminescence of Eu3+ ions under 284 nm excitation and the blue fluorescence of CDs under 354 nm excitation (Fig. 4cd). At the excitation wavelength of 395 nm, the KLM: Eu3+@CDs composites emits white luminescence, whose emission spectrum is constituted by the broad emission band of CDs and the narrow emission bands of Eu3+ ion (Fig. 4e). The CIE chromaticity diagram shows the luminous color of KLM: Eu3+@CDs excited at 284 nm, 354 nm, and 395 nm, which are in the range of blue, white, and orange red, respectively (Fig. 4b). Such interesting multi-mode response characteristics endows KLM: Eu3+@CDs, with important potential for applications in advanced security encryption.

A possible luminous mechanism of KLM: Eu3+@CDs was shown in Fig. 5. The FT-IR analysis confirmed that the surface of CDs possessed multifarious functional groups which could be used as sub-fluorophores to produce luminescence, such as -NH2, -OH, PO and CN. The luminescence of CDs is associated with their low electron vibrational energy levels [40]. When the CDs is irradiated by UV lamp, these functional groups absorb energy and excite the CDs to the excited state (Sn), and the electrons in Sn fell into the lowest excited singlet state (S1) by nonradiative relaxation [41]. Some of the carbon nuclei radiated in the form of blue fluorescence, and some of them passed from S1 to the triplet state (T1) in intersystem crossing (ISC) [42]. It is possible that the surface functional groups of KLM and CDs in the composite combine with each other to make the triplet state less stable. Then CDs returns to the ground state (S0) by radiation-free relaxation, so there is no longer a green afterglow. In the KLM: Eu3+, the sample can effectively absorb 284 nm high-energy ultraviolet light due to the existence of Eu-O charge transfer band. The energy is transferred in non-radiative form to 5G2 (376 nm, 381 nm), 5L6 and 5D2 of Eu3+. These energy levels can also be directly excited by 365 nm UV lamp. Eu3+ relaxes the internal energy level to the 5D0 energy level by radiation-free relaxation, resulting in the red characteristic luminescence of 5D0-7F1, 7F2, 7F3 and 7F4, respectively [43]. Because of CDs are embedded in the silicon shell, which is thin enough to allow light to pass through, on the surface of KLM: Eu3+@CDs composites, the red emission of Eu3+ ions and the blue emission of CDs can be simultaneously retained in the composites. When the luminescent intensity of CDs and Eu3+ ions is matched, the white light emission can be achieved at 395 nm by which the excitation bands of them overlap here.

Next, in order to obtain the best white luminescence, a series of KLM: Eu3+@CDs composites were synthesized by adjusting the ratios of CDs and KLM, with the ratio of KLM to CDs ranging from 1:2 to 10:1. Fig. 6a shows the excitation spectra of KLM: Eu3+@CDs composites at 395 nm. With increasing concentration of KLM: Eu3+, the luminescent intensity of Eu3+ ions gradually increase, and the emission peak of CDs gradually decreases. Corresponding their luminescent colors to the chromaticity coordinate diagram as shown in Fig. 6b. It can be seen that the luminescent color of samples gradually transitions from blue to orange-red, and the sample with a ratio of 2:1 is exactly in the white range, which can be used to prepare white LEDs. In addition, different proportions of composite materials can emit "warm", "neutral" and "cold" white light as shown in the inset of Fig. 6b, which can be applied to the production of color-adjustable LEDs. Then we prepared white LEDs by coating KLM: Eu3+@CDs (mass ratio 2:1) as phosphor on the surface of the 395 nm UV chip. As shown in the Fig. 7b, after 5A DC is turned on, the device emits strong white light, and the luminous color is in the white area in the CIE chromaticity diagram. Therefore, the KLM: Eu3+@CDs composites has great research and application prospect in the direction of practical lighting.

With the development of the times, information security has become more and more concerned. Due to the poor security of traditional monochrome information encryption, multi-mode information encryption has become the focus of attention. The KLM: Eu3+@CDs composites show red, blue, and white emission modes under the excitation of 284 nm, 354 nm and 395 nm light sources, respectively. Benefited from the molybdate luminescent feature of KLM: Eu3+@CDs, a multilevel encryption system was displayed. When the composite material is used with KLM: Eu3+ and CDs, three colors of red, blue, and white can be freely combined to realize multi-mode information encryption. KLM: Eu3+, CDs and KLM: Eu3+@CDs were mixed with polyacrylic acid (PAA) at a mass ratio of 1:2 and combined to form a white "888" pattern according to the scheme shown in Fig. 7b. Since the sample has three light-emitting colors, we simply encode the pattern, that is, select the first number in red, the second number in blue and the third number in white as effective information. When the pattern is excited by 284 nm, 354 nm and 395 nm light sources, it shows three different luminescent patterns of red, blue and white, respectively. For the three colors of the pattern information is disordered, but in accordance with the order of red, blue, white selection, the true information will be obtained as "404" (Fig. 7c). According to this idea of encryption of information, the data will have a strong encryption effect.

In summary, an effective and facile strategy was described to prepare polychromatic dynamic light-emitting materials by encapsulating CDs on the surface of KLM: Eu3+. The encapsulation of CDs in SiO2 can effectively avoid the fluorescence quenching induced by polymerization, so that CDs can still emit complete independent fluorescence in the composites. According to the overlapping (395 nm) of excitation bands of CDs and Eu3+ ion, the material can emit white light tuned by blue and red light, so it can be used as a new white LED phosphor. In addition, the difference of excitation band between CDs and Eu3+ ion and Eu-O charge transfer band shows unique red, blue, and white emission, which can be used in multi-mode information encryption applications, making the hidden information difficult to be copied or pirated. All the results have certain guiding significance for the reasonable design of white LED and multiple information encryption.

Ziqian Lv: Formal analysis, Investigation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Hanyu Xu: Funding acquisition, Investigation, Supervision. Zhiying Wang: Conceptualization, Software, Writing – review & editing. Mochen Jia: Conceptualization, Software, Writing – review & editing. Yanling Wei: Validation, Visualization. Zuoling Fu: Validation, Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the National Science Foundation of China (no. 11904125 and 11874182), partially supported by Science and Technology Project of the 13th Five-Year Plan of Jilin Provincial Department of Education (No. JJKH20190179KJ) and sponsored by Doctoral Project (BSKJ201925), gratefully acknowledge the assistance of Instrument and equipment sharing platform, the college of physics.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Physically unclonable cryptographic primitives using self-assembled carbon nanotubes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Hu, Z.; Comeras, J.; Park, H.; Tang, J.; Afzali, A.; Tulevski, G.S.; Hannon, J.B.; Liehr, M.; Han, S.J., Physically unclonable cryptographic primitives using self-assembled carbon nanotubes. Nat. Nanotechnol. 2016, 11 (6), 559–565. https://doi.org/10.1038/nnano.2016.1

Deng, L.; Deng, J.; Guan, Z.; Tao, J.; Chen, Y.; Yang, Y.; Zhang, D.; Tang, J.; Li, Z.; Li, Z.; Yu, S.; Zheng, G.; Xu, H.; Qiu, C.W.; Zhang, S., Malus-metasurface-assisted polarization multiplexing. Light: Sci. Appl. 2020, 9, 101. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41377–020-0327–7

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Time-dependent phosphorescence colors from carbon dots for advanced dynamic information encryption

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Tan, J.; Li, Q.; Meng, S.; Li, Y.; Yang, J.; Ye, Y.; Tang, Z.; Qu, S.; Ren, X., Time-Dependent Phosphorescence Colors from Carbon Dots for Advanced Dynamic Information Encryption. Adv. Mater. 2021, 33 (16), e2006781. https://doi.org/10.1002/adma.202006781

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Low-dose real-time X-ray imaging with nontoxic double perovskite scintillators

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhu, W.; Ma, W.; Su, Y.; Chen, Z.; Chen, X.; Ma, Y.; Bai, L.; Xiao, W.; Liu, T.; Zhu, H.; Liu, X.; Liu, H.; Liu, X.; Yang, Y., Low-dose real-time X-ray imaging with nontoxic double perovskite scintillators. Light: Sci. Appl. 2020, 9, 112 (1). https://doi.org/10.1038/s41377–020-00353–0
